Hi, I'm Orin Claff and you're in the Pitch Mastery training and we are starting to work through the Strong Method. And the S in Strong is set the frame. And of course Strong is set the frame, tell the story, reveal the intrigue, offer the prize, nail the hook point and get the deal. And the first thing we have to do is really go deep into frames so we know how to set the frame. Setting the frame is the foundation for going into a pitch and gives you lots and lots of ways to control and tools to get the results you want. So you'll hear me say this many times over the course of the training that all social interactions are governed by frames. And of course business meetings or business calls or even online interactions, email or websites are social interactions. Let's get some definition around frames so you really understand it. So frames are a method the brain uses to keep control over the massive flow of information coming in from your senses. So we know we have five senses and everybody's familiar with those. We've seen the movie The Sixth Sense, we all probably have a sixth sense. But what's not discussed is there's another 15 senses, nerve endings and lots of biological senses. We have a massive flow of information coming into our brain, visual, auditory, sensory. And getting control of these is the job of frames. It's how your mind gets control of the massive inflow of information. In the physical world, frames do two things that help you survive and definitely to get from the beginning of the day to the end of the day alive and with business deals. When I say you get to the end of the day alive, it's because remember we're descendants of people who had just three emotions, angry, hungry and horny. That's everything. So the way they interpreted their world was with frames. Our ancestors had to run around all day through the African savanna looking for something to eat and someone to mate with and somewhere warm to sleep at night. So frames give you the ability to focus on the most important things and the situations around you and not anything else. Sometimes I'll go like this. You have seen painters do that. They're framing the landscape. So frames give you a way to the ability to focus on the most important situation around you and not on anything else. So there's a lot more you need to know about frames but this is a very good starting point. Let's use this example so you can understand it in use. Let's say you walk into a restaurant, something we've all done and your frame initially is the entire place. That means as you're absorbing information from everything that's happening around you. Waiters are bustling around. There's dozens of tables and diners and clanking of dishes and conversations and kitchen doors are slamming open and shut and a husband and wife are arguing and there's a table with three kids throwing food and 50 simultaneous conversations are hitting your auditory tracks and there's a huge visual and auditory and sensory frame but you're just trying to find one person in all this movement and information. It's very hard and we've all experienced this. You're supposed to be meeting someone. You can't find them even though they're sitting 10 or 15 in front of you and maybe sometimes waving their arm. This is a good example of way too much information coming into your senses much too quickly. You'll start to frame that. You'll start to hone in and ignore certain information so you know that your friend isn't at a table with three kids and that will get framed out. You know they're not a waiter and you know they don't have babies and you know they're not part of a big group and you know they're not old and you know they're not very young and eventually the frame will develop and you'll be able to focus on the person you're there to meet. So the single most important feature of frames and we'll talk about it in a great deal. Once a frame is set new information bounces off of it without bothering you. In fact all frames have a strength and in every social interaction the stronger frame overrides the weaker one and it wins. Let me talk about this frame strength in terms of the restaurant example. Once you hone in on your friend that you're supposed to meet and you start walking towards that table there's no new information that is going to distract you. You're going to walk towards that table, you're going to meet your friend and you're going to sit down. So there can be more babies and more couples fighting and more waiters and waiters dropping dishes and crazy stuff going on. But it's not going to break the frame that you have set. Once a frame is set and has meaning new information does not break it. So let's talk about meaning for a minute. Let's just say you're going to meet a casual friend but all of a sudden you see out of the corner of your eye and you focus your attention somewhere else and it's a business associate that you've been wanting to do a million dollar deal with. So now that has stronger meaning and that can reset your frame and you'll focus on that guy and you'll go over and now your friend says hey I thought we were going to meet and you're there with a guy and give you a million dollar deal. The information of your friend calling you over will bounce off of your new frame because it has stronger meaning. So frames have meaning once the meaning is strongly set then almost no new information will break the frame. There's a technical term for this. It's called belief perseverance. It means that after information is framed that frame has meaning. It becomes very very strong. It perseveres. An easy way to think about this is that initial judgments are hard to shake. You hear that a lot. First impressions are lasting. Initial judgments are hard to shake. So you already know about frames because you know about this. First impressions are so important you might have told yourself that or your friends or you act that way. So you know that frames have a lot of application in the real world. We're going to go deeper and teach you really very very strong applications of framing. We're going to get that in a minute. You know already that for example projects have time frames and pictures have frames around them and houses are framed up. So framing refers to how things are constrained and how things are packaged. For example a red frame on a painting calls attention to the red in it. Or framing calls attention to things and makes other things not in focus. Let's define frames more precisely. And I would say it this way. Frames are mental views that you use to simplify complex issues. Frames are mental views that you use to simplify complex issues. Frames contain some information and purposely exclude other information. So it creates a viewpoint. A frame is the way an argument or an issue or a set of facts are presented. Because there's no more clear definition around frames. I'll give that again. A frame is a way that an argument, an issue or a set of facts are presented. So frames make information accessible. That they have very, very powerful control over meetings and both business and personal. So all meetings in my perspective and if you've read material you know this are social encounters. All meetings are social encounters. And so when two people come together in a business meeting it's social. And when two people come together they both bring a frame. The frame as we've talked about is their point of view. It's their perspective. It's all the information they have about their side of the deal. So if I go to meet you and I bring my frame on the thing I'm trying to sell you, my point of view is that it's worth $5 million because I know everything about it. That's the frame I'm bringing to the situation. The frame you're bringing is that you may or may not need it. And you're not ready to buy it. You're not even sure you want it. And in your frame it's worth $1 million. So when frames come together they are incompatible. They come to dominant frames or perspectives or points of view cannot be in the same place at the same time. It cannot be that my point of view is that it's worth $5 million and your point of view is that it's worth $1 million. Those perspectives cannot be in the same business meeting at the same time. One frame has got to become the dominant frame. So frames come together and they crash into each other and they collide and they crumple each other like a slow moving car accident and they bend and morph and they battle for dominance. And eventually one frame breaks the other to pieces and it absorbs the frame. And so stronger frames absorb weaker ones and become the dominant perspective. But it has to be this way. You cannot have two dominant perspectives in the same business deal, in the same meetings. So meetings are social encounters. Each person brings their own frame. Meetings come together, they collide, they have crumple zones. Eventually one frame breaks the other and it becomes the dominant. The other becomes the supplicant. The dominant frame absorbs the weaker and becomes the point of view for the social interaction. And all meetings work this way. And you'll begin to observe this when you start to look for it. But let me give you the tools. So when you're seeing people come together you can see this happening. And for a while you'll visualize. Here it comes and here comes the other one. Oh, I just saw him collide. That was painful. Here we go. That one's breaking the other one. Now it's absorbing it. Oh, I just saw that guy get frame control. That was cool and amazing. You have a way of experiencing the world that you didn't have previously. And it's a very fun way to begin to look at social and business interactions. So there's five frames that you need to know. And these are ones I use every single day. The prize frame, which we talked a little bit about in the introductory material, but I'll go through it now. So the prize frame happens in every business meeting. One person views themselves as the prize. So if I am the buyer, I view myself as the prize because I have money. And if you are the seller, you typically view yourself as the supplicant, as the perfect former, trying to win the prize. So it's very important to know who is the prize in a social interaction in a business meeting and to try and become the prize. We all want to be the prize that other people are trying to win. We're going to go into that in real depth, but that's the basics of it. So the other frame that I use every single day, and you will as well, is the time frame because time frames drive decision making. Now I'll say this over and over. Amtrak, the train leaves at 3.03, doesn't leave at 3.04, so you have to decide where they're going to be on it. The train leaves the station at 3.03. If you're on it, you get to go where the train is going. And if you're not, then you get to stay where you are and not participate. So we use time frames to drive decision making. So it's very important to learn how to set time constraints. We're going to go deep into that. So these frames you'll soon be using with greater control than maybe you use them today. Cool thing is you already know and use prize frames and time frames. I'll give you a quick example. We know as humans things that we try and get training for. So for example, people go to karate and they go to defense training and they learn lots of moves. But I went to a training program last weekend in Las Vegas. And they showed us a great video, which was two street gang guys fighting in the street and they're hitting each other, punching each other, and they're grappling on the ground and no one's really winning. They're switching back and forth, not really doing damage, but they're just fighting. And it's interesting. Then all of a sudden out of the frame comes a large overweight, out of condition woman. And she kicks one of the guys right in the head and he flies off and is completely incapacitated. And the instructor made the point, she has no training whatsoever. And she just did a better job in that fight than any of the participants. So we know how to do things as humans without much training. And in the same way that she ended that fight in a second with no martial arts or military or police training because she knew the natural things to do, we know how to use prize frames and we know how to use time frames. We're just going to learn here how to deploy the things we already know how to do in very high stakes social and business circumstances. The power frame is a little bit different. It's not as much that you're going to use it, it's that you're going to recognize it. Once you recognize it, know how to combat it. The power frame we have all experienced. We walk into a room or get on the phone and the other guy has the money and he has the decision making power. He uses that power to try and get economic concession from us. The power frame is what people will use to try and get you to do stuff, to lower your price or to give free samples or to spend your time for them without giving you economic benefit. So the power frame has to be combated. We're going to go deep into how to do that and more definition with the power frame. When you walk into a room, very simply, the other guy is leaning back and saying, show me what you have so I can make a decision with all my money if I'm going to buy it or give you a purchase order or work with you. Then you have just walked into the power frame and it's time to get to work and take it away. The longer you leave a power frame in existence, the more it's going to demand economic concession from you. We'll go into exactly what that means. Another one is the analyst frame. You're not going to use this because it's mean and it sucks. The more you use it, the less people are going to like you. The analyst frame is when you get very, very detailed questions from the other side. It is when the other side wants information from you that is void of social relationship. You've heard this before. Just give us the information. Don't worry about explaining everything. Don't worry about the meaning. Send over the information. Give us the information and we'll decide over on our side. That's the analyst frame. It's very disruptive. When you see information requests that are moving faster than the development of the relationship, you are losing frame control and you're losing the ability to frame and provide meaning to the information you are giving. I'll say it again. When you are getting information requests that are moving faster than the development of the relationship, you are losing frame control. Now I'm going to give you a chart where you can, that charts out the typical development of a business relationship or a deal and you're going to be able to chart how much information requests are coming at you and see if it's out of phase with the typical deal. You'll know when the analyst frame is coming at you and then what to do with it. If you feel the information request is out of phase with where the relationship is, then you've got an analyst frame. You've got to do something about it. These are frames you'll learn to recognize and deal with. If you don't recognize them and you don't deal with them, you'll reduce your chances of selling your product or getting a deal. Or if you do in fact get a deal, you will get a lower price. The power frame and the analyst frame have got to be dealt with or you will pay for it. Why do you think I spent 10,000 hours dealing with this stuff? There's much more fun things to do. Surfing, I live by the beach. I could be a great surfer. I could be a great soccer player, maybe not great, but good. Or I could be a stamp collector or something fun. I invested my time to do this because this is how you lose money and lose control of deals. Power frames and analyst frames make you lose control of deals and get less price for what you want to sell. I said there were five frames. We had the prize frame and the time frame. You're going to learn how to use those to get frame control. Then there's the power frame and the analyst frame. You're going to look for those coming at you so you can defend against it and defend your price and get a deal done. There's a fifth frame that's a little bit different. It's the needy frame, the needy frame. Unlike the power frame and the analyst frame, which you will learn to recognize in others, the needy frame you will learn to recognize in yourself. I'll say it again. The needy frame you will learn to recognize in yourself. Then you get the tools to deal with it and eradicate neediness. Neediness destroys deals. Try it. Go in your next deal and say, Mr. Byer, Mr. Investor, Mr. JV Partner, I need this deal. Seriously, I need it. I want it. It's not that I want it. I need it. Otherwise, I'm going to have real problems on my side. I want you to give this to me because I need it. If you try that, please don't. If you do try that, you're going to see they're going to run away and the deal's over. Neediness destroys deals. You're going to eradicate neediness within yourself. I'm not saying replace it with bravado or aggression or alpha behavior. What I'm saying is you need to be response-independent and not needy. I'm going to show you how to do that. These are frames you need to know and learn how to use, learn how to recognize, and soon you will. Soon you're going to be using frames like the time frame. When somebody says to you, well, I only have 30 minutes to meet, you're going to say, really? Because I only have 25 minutes. We better start right now. They say, well, then I only have 20 minutes. You're going to say, then I have 19. This is some of the games you're going to play, fun games. Not silly games, but fun games. When you're using frames in live-action fire. If somebody says to me, hey, I'm only going to give you 60 minutes to present, I'm going to say to them, I present in 20 minutes. For the other 40 minutes, then I'm going to allow you to present to me. You're going to use time frames in live-action fire. You also need to know how to set frames firmly in place. There's using frames, but they also need to be set so they can't be easily moved or changed. When you set a frame properly, you have a strong foundation to present from. I'm going to do that for you right now so you can see it in example, exactly the way I would do it. We're going to do set three frames. The prize frame, the time frame, and the big idea frame, which we haven't talked a lot about yet, but you will be using. Watch as I set the prize frame, the time frame, and the big idea frame. Watch how it is being set and you'll know exactly what to do for yourself. First, I'm going to set the big idea. Hear me out. If you walk into a building or a house or an apartment while talking on your cell phone, you will get signal disruption. The signal will degrade. That's the way it works. You've experienced it. In your life, once the signal goes out, you go to a landline. Maybe you pull up Skype. If you could build a semiconductor in the way that we are working on that doesn't get signal disruption, then one billion people who do not have landlines will want your product. Then you could build a billion-dollar company in two to three years. That's the big idea. Minimize signal disruption through a better semiconductor and sell it to a billion people. The big idea. Now you've seen a frame get set very, very firmly. First I'll set the prize frame. Because we have a working prototype of this semiconductor and we also have advanced orders from France Telecom, AT&T, and Verizon, we are not seeking out investors. We're going to take investment, but we're interviewing investors. We're going to find the right ones that we like and we'll allow them to come into our deal and be our partners. That's the prize frame being set very, very firmly. Next, I'll set the time frame. In the next 30 days, we will have selected the investor who we think adds the most value to our deal. Then we'll go forward with that partner together. Please prepare yourself for a different process than maybe what you're used to. Prepare yourself to explain your value add just beyond just your money. Explain what value you bring to us. That will help us select you as the investors that we're going to allow to give us money. Now you just saw three frames being set very, very firmly. Hopefully you had a sense that they're almost immovable the way that I've set them. You're going to use frames in dynamic situations to get control of social environments, but going into meetings, you're also going to use frames to set the context and set what you're willing to do and what you're not willing to do and what the time constraint is. Time frames drive decision making, prize frames drive who is reacting to who, and the big idea frame sets exactly what the stakes are. We'll be using those. When you frame correctly, you're looking for certain results. I said it was fun and we do do this for fun and it's great when people are reacting to us. The way you know that you've set frames correctly, you're looking for certain results to happen. Again in this martial arts training, we learned this defense training. You hit somebody properly, you don't have to guess. You're looking for a certain reaction to them. When they bend over and throw their arms out, you know you've kicked them in the nuts as well as it can be kicked. It can cause damage because you see this. That's exactly what humans do when they get kicked there. I want to describe the results you're looking for, the equivalent, so you know that you're getting frame control and deal control. The first result you're looking for is very easy to recognize. It's just energy. It's just a feeling that people are paying attention to you. You know what this feels like. That they're interested in the next thing that you're going to say. It's that sense of, some people call it flow, that it's dynamically, things are going in your direction, the things you say are being paid attention to, and you'll feel that. Definitely, you won't see people looking at their phones or writing distractively or talking to each other. If you see those signs, you definitely do not have frame control. The second thing is people are reacting to you. Here's a good way to look at it. If you say something mildly humorous, a casual joke, and people chuckle and laugh and give you a sense that they enjoy the humor that you are giving, they're reacting to you. You know that you're getting frame control and you're seeing some of the reactions that you want. By contrary, if they tell a joke and you feel obligated to laugh at it, even if it's not that funny, then they have frame control on you. You're reacting to them. Who is reacting to who? When you see people reacting to you, the things you say and do, then you know this is a result that you want. Number three, there's authentic participation at some level. They're not just listening. They're participating. They're adding some kind of small value to the conversation. They're giving and taking. When you see authentic participation, not informational questions, but participation, then you've got a result that you're looking for. You've got frame control. I see a problem with this when people are asking questions, getting answers. Asking questions, getting answers. Very common. It's not participation. It's just question, answer, question, answer, answer, question. When you have that, there's no frame control in this situation. You're not getting what you want. Authentic participation is what you want. Fourth thing is the buyer or the audience or the guy you're trying to sell your ideas to is starting to give some value to you. He's saying, hey, I know Donald Trump's group. I know Steve Wynn. I know Bill Gates or whoever is relevant in your business. I should connect you to them because this could be valuable to them too. I'd like to get their ideas on this product or idea and bring them into the conversation. When they're providing value and contributing and making this a situation where both people are providing value, then you've got frame control and it's working very well. The other thing you see is a very healthy tension in the social dynamic. It's not just information, question. Question information, as I've said before. There's some kidding. I don't believe that. Why don't you explain that to me a different way or some of this prizing behavior which we talked about? I'm trying to think of a good reason why I would let you be my customer. That's a healthy tension in the social dynamic. You're going to look for some of this jostling back and forth. Maybe if your buyer says, well, I kind of like your product, but I probably need to go look at 10 or 15 other people who sell this kind of thing and see if they're as good as you. If he says that in a shrill, bitter, mean way, that's not a healthy tension. But if he says that with a smile on his face and he doesn't really mean it, he's just teasing you. That's a healthy social dynamic and that's what you're looking for so you know you've got frame control. This is my favorite. This is how I know I'm doing an amazing job. There are just weird reactions from people. You know you're doing a great job when they spill their coffee and they tell inappropriate stories. They're trying to prove themselves to you socially. If this happens, you're doing a really great job. Many times I will give a pitch and I'll include in it the story. We're in S of strong setting the frame and T is tell the story, the thing you'll learn next. But when I tell a great story and I give people a chance to tell their own story, what will happen is they'll try and do it as well as I do and they get frazzled and they'll say weird and inappropriate things because they don't know how to do it. They're trying to mimic what you're doing but they don't know what you're doing. The second they say something weird and inappropriate, they spill their coffee and then they mop it up and then they're trying to come back from that. You should just lean back and say when this happens, I have frame control. You already know how to set and use frames. I'm just expanding your application of the things that you know how to do. Next, we're going to move into the specifics of time frames. I have said many times, framing is one of the most powerful things you will ever learn that you can use in business, get deals done, sell stuff, raise your pitch to close ratio because that means more revenue. That's what we all want. That's what we care so much about this stuff. We want to, every time we pitch, we want to close. If we pitch 10 times and today we close five, then next month we want to close six times and the following month seven times. This is all about raising your pitch to close ratio, just doing better. Let's go into some practical applications of pricing, how to prize, how to recognize the analyst's frame and what to do when you recognize it, and third, how to deal with the power frame and get control of it, get under control so it doesn't cost you money. Let's start with pricing. We said before there's ways to recognize when you're doing it well. One way that I believe in is you will see and measure your own reaction to people when they say something negative about your deal. If you hear anything negative or experience some power framing on yourself or somebody trying to commoditize what you have or any reference to your deal as being weak or not important, whenever I get that, I close distance and shut it down. It's an instant and immediate reaction from me. Let me explain what I mean by these two things. Navy SEALs that I work with, they use this term close distance when they see a threat, like during an operation, and they see a threat that they recognize and know what it is, a guy with a gun, a guy with a bomb, whatever, a guy driving a truck with bad stuff in it. During an operation when they see that and they know what it is, they don't evaluate the threat. They don't check satellite data and do little red circles and they don't call people in Washington and say, hey, what do you think we should do? When they see a threat that they're familiar with, they close distance and shut it down. Shutting it down is exactly what it sounds like. Terminate. When you hear something about yourself or your deal that indicates you're not the prize, you need to close distance and aggressively shut it down. Give us some very specific examples. If I hear, what you have is kind of interesting. I immediately react to that. It's not kind of interesting. It's very interesting. We didn't travel all this distance to show you something kind of interesting and you didn't set this meeting and spend hours to come here because it's kind of interesting. This stuff is amazing. If you hear something like, this is a lot like, this is a lot like whatever, whatever, then you're going to immediately close distance on that and shut it down. You say, no, it's not like anything. What we have is unique, very well differentiated. That's why you came here and that's why you're interested in it. In any way, I'm not like anybody else you've ever met. I know this stuff better than anybody else in this business. This is very, very well differentiated. You're going to love it. Otherwise, you wouldn't be here. If you hear something like, it's amazing that you built a product in this niche and you can close distance and shut that down. Really? Divorce is not a niche. There's 100 million people either in divorce or thinking about it or having problems with their marriage. It's not a niche. It's a great product in the big sector, an emerging market. Very exciting. No niche around here. I don't know what you're looking at. I'm walking through it. If you hear something like this, your product is something we're evaluating. Then of course, you're going to close distance and shut it down. You're going to say, there's some confusion here. Actually, we're evaluating you. You're not evaluating us. You're going to move on from that. Especially if you hear something like this, well, you have to talk to our purchasing department. I'm warning you, they are tough. Then you're going to say, well, I think you should call them up and warn them that we're tough. That would probably be a better use of your time. We're tough. Warn them, those bastards. When you hear something that tries to reframe you as not the prize or indicate any kind of weakness or softness, close distance, shut it down. These are very different from what I call no fit statements. You cannot attack someone. You cannot close distance, shut down when they don't have a need for what you have. When somebody says, we just bought one. We have no money left. We don't have a budget. It's beyond our reach. It's too big. It's too small. It's too ugly. You can't argue that. That's not pricing. You can't attack that or reframe it. If it's not for them, it's not for them. There's other ways to deal with that in the grand architecture. It's mainly not to be in front of those people. If somebody says, it's too ugly, we don't buy these kind of ugly things, like it might be the four seasons and you're selling polyester lawn chairs, you can see it's just not a fit. It's too ugly. They're not going to buy it. Once you find that out, there's no amount of framing that's going to make the four seasons by polyester-lined lawn chairs. When you do hear somebody indicate weakness, get on top of that and don't let it continue. We'll give you all of these in the workbook so you can see them written down one by one. We've asked before, how do you know when you're successfully pricing? Here's an example. You hear people say something like this, oh, we might select you if you give a good presentation and then immediately within yourself, you know that's wrong and you reply, I think you should worry less about selecting us and more about proving to us that we would want to work with you. When you immediately know to say that, I'll repeat it. Guys, I think you should worry less about selecting us and more about proving to us that we would want to work with you. Now you're pricing and you've got it. That should rise up within you. Anytime somebody says, demonstrate your worth to us, you should immediately know, wait a second, I'm the prize. You guys need to demonstrate your worth to me. Can't say it enough. Pricing, it's not puffery. You're not blowing up your chest and you're not peacocking. It's not some kind of internal motivation. It's you acknowledging your own value to yourself first, then you can do it to other people. It's not motivational. It's not getting pumped up for the big fight. I'm going to kill that guy with my right. It's not about internal motivation. It's really understanding your value and then making your value the prize other people have to win. Here's how you build and strengthen your prize frame. Build it. This is in the workbook but we'll do it now. You're going to take out a piece of paper. You can do it on the fly if you don't have the materials with you. Three columns, column one. What do I have? Column two, what do they need? That's all good and well. It sounds like basic sales planning. For a prize and you need a third column, where am I dominant? What do I have? What do they need? Where am I dominant? I'll give you an example. You can all relate to. Let's say I sell blue plastic frogs. What do I have? I've got blue plastic frogs. They have three gigawatts of insect zapping power. I myself have five years of training in insect destruction. I know this industry very well. I have lots of industry knowledge in white papers and I know all the players and I'm very familiar with the industry. The other thing I have is my frogs are expensive. We have fast delivery time but they are expensive. That's what I have. What I have is not what do I think other people want? What do I really have, both my pros and my cons? What do I have? What do they need? What does the customer need to hear? They need dead insects. They need a really, really high quality sales person. They need protection from buying bad products because there's lots of weird blue plastic frogs in the market. If you don't buy it properly, then you're going to lose a lot of money. They need protection from bad buying. They need market insight because this is stuff you really have to understand how to use it once you have it and get it right. They need good pricing. That's what they want. That's what they need. Where am I dominant? I don't have everything they need. I don't have good pricing. Where am I dominant to all of this? For me, my integrity is one of the greatest things I have. The other sales people in the industry just aren't as integral as I am. I have more industry knowledge than the next guy. We have very fast delivery and we just happen to have very high power zapping. That's what we have. In my presentation, say Mr. Buyer, I'm here to sell you these blue plastic frogs and here's how the market is changing. I'm the prize and you're the supplicant and you've got to earn your way into my deal. We do all these kinds of things and I get rid of the power frame and I bump their analysts out of the room and I do everything right. I'm still going to hear stuff. I'm still going to hear from them, well, we're thinking about the price. Your price is not that good. Then my prize frame is going to kick in. I say, hey, that's good. If pricing is the most important to you, go get it somewhere else. If all you care about is pricing among these things where we're dominant, then we are right for each other. Price, price is not how you successfully get dead insects. It's just how you save money. In other businesses, price works good. You buy rice by price, go ahead. But in this business, we need results, dead insects. You can't do it by price. If price was the key determinant of success, we would do it that way. We would make this stuff cheaper. You can't win with cheap blue plastic frogs the right way to win in this market. You get someone like me who knows what they're doing. It makes price irrelevant. You pay to get me because I help you buy this stuff correctly. These things are complicated. If you don't buy them through somebody with experience that you can trust, you get bad results. You don't get dead insects during the farming season. You get lots of supply that you don't need. You get control units that don't work. If you want to focus on price, you got to go somewhere else because we have great delivery, which is what you need to succeed in this market. We have myself and I know everything about this stuff. You can trust me. Those are dimensions that you should care about because that's how you get results. When you hear yourself saying things like that, then when somebody questions you on price or points at some weakness, then you're going to immediately prize yourself in those dimensions where you're dominant. Now we're going to do something that I love, which is dealing with the analyst frame because it was one of the most difficult things for me to solve. You're sitting there making a presentation around the phone and an analyst comes on, starts asking you all these very specific and difficult questions. Every time they ask you something, you stop giving your presentation and are answering factual questions. It's very disruptive. Years that cannot figure out. A, how to recognize it early enough and B, how to deal with it. That's what we're going to do right now is learn how to recognize it and learn how to effectively deal with it. The analyst frame is recognized when you see the buyer or the buyer's team doing deep due diligence, asking for lots of information, getting factual polls before there are three things in place. Before there is wanting. If they're asking for lots and lots of information before they've exhibited that they want what you have, they want it. They're willing to work to get it. It's the analyst frame you see coming at you when there's more and more information requests before they have wanting. Number two, before there's a healthy, collaborative and working relationship with you and the other side. If you're getting lots of information requests where there's a healthy, collaborative, working relationship, then you know you're dealing with the analyst frame and it has got to be put down. Three, if you're getting lots and lots of information requests before there are basic deal terms in place, before they're exhibiting wanting but also before they know roughly what the price is, roughly what the delivery timelines are, roughly what they're going to get. They're asking for lots and lots of stuff, information, reports, spreadsheets, data, contracts, all the stuff, customer reviews, access into the website, seeing the code. These are all information polls. If the basic deal terms haven't been discussed and they're asking for all that information, you know the analyst frame. It's coming at you. Let me give you the most basic example of the analyst frame taking place. You hear this. This looks interesting. Why don't you send us a proposal for us to review? That is the analyst frame in full effect. Of course, you want to deframe it and reframe it as quickly as possible. When somebody asks me for a proposal, my temperature goes from one to 10 immediately. That's the biggest waste of time. I'm going to send you a proposal, spend hours writing something out, email it to you for you to read over there. It's not even clear that you want it. We don't have a working relationship. What sense is that? You know the analyst frame is coming at you. When the information requests you're getting are out of phase with the buying process. Let's go into that and make it very specific. In the worksheet and we'll put it up for you, what a typical buying process is, at least in my business. Yours will be a slight adjustment from this, but it will be similar. In phase one, you're going to introduce your product and your ideas to the customer. Phase one is to introduce your product and your ideas to the customer. Phase one. In phase two, you're going to establish buying timelines and basic terms and pricing bookends. Phase two. Terms, timelines, rough pricing. Phase three, you're going to explore options, product options and how you might work together. Now there's some real work happening. You're going to discuss competitive offerings. They're going to say, hey, we're thinking about buying a John Deere and yours is made in China. How do you guys deal with that? You're going to overcome these basic objections and you're going to establish compatibility between you and them. If it's a larger sale where there's a company to company, you're going to get connectivity between the two organizations. Your CFO is going to talk to their CFO. Your engineers are going to be talking to their engineers. Your salespeople are going to be talking to their buyers. Your CEO is going to be talking to their CEO. There's compatible people in your organization are going to connect to the compatible people in their organization. It's called establishing counterparts. In this phase, there's going to be momentum. It's just going to feel like the wheels of progress are grinding. Everything's not going perfectly, but it's moving forward. Two steps forward, one step back. Three steps forward, two steps back. Then hopefully three steps forward and just that's it, three steps forward. There's momentum. In phase four, now we go into information polls. There's testing and deep analytics in your product. They want spreadsheets or they want test data or they want customer reviews or they want the white paper or they want more and more detail. It's kind of okay here. You're in phase four. You're almost closed. Here you should be seeing high quality due diligence. They want to review contracts that you have. They want to visit your factory or test the product. Field testing. You're going to see some real due diligence here. In phase five, the closing timeline is established. Everybody knows when they're going to pay and when they're going to get delivery. People have each other's cell phone numbers. Emails are going back and forth late at night. It just feels like the closing is real and it's going to happen. Those are the five phases of a deal happening. Might be faster in your business. Might be slower. But it's going to be something similar. When the amount of due diligence and information requests and document polls or questions that you are getting are out of phase with these five progressive steps, these five phases, without when they're out of phase with this sequence, you have an analyst frame on your hands and there are many fixes to this and you will want to fix it. The basic frames to use to fix the analyst frame to get control over it. One is a timeframe. You could say just like this. Guys, all this information requesting, all this stuff that you want, this is going to chew up time when we haven't gotten accomplished the basic milestones for this phase of the deal. Just like that. You're going to call it what it is. This is going to chew up time when we haven't gotten more important things done. Who can argue with that? You might say it exactly like this. Guys, why waste our time going through specifications, looking at code, looking at contracts, doing infield tests when we don't even know if we want to or can do business together. It's exactly so you use a timeframe. This is wasting time. You may pull a power frame of your own to deal with the analyst frame. I might say it like this. Getting lots of information requests too early in the process, I might say, hey, look, until you prove to me that you're a good customer, and by a good customer I mean you don't break the equipment, you reorder parts when you're supposed to. Until that happens, I'm paying to be your supplier. Paying to be your supplier. I have to spend money to earn you as a customer and support you until I get a break even. If you want my money, I'm going to need you to do a couple of things. First of all, throw this analyst out of here. He's wasting my time. You might not say it as aggressively as that, but that's the structure that you're going to want to use. You're using my money. I'm investing in this process as much as you are. Yeah, you might be the customer, but I'm putting money to work here too. I'm going to waste my time with analytical questions until we know more about whether you're going to be a good customer. You're going to do that. This is very useful. This next thing, the value frame. The value frame. When you see the analyst frame coming at you and you're getting all these information requests, I think you can say very credibly, we'd like to have the analysts here with us today on my side as well and on your side, add value to the deal. This kind of due diligence that you guys are asking for, this information that you're asking for can be done in breakout sessions. We don't need myself and yourself, management teams here, our teams. This can really be done in breakout sessions or by email. We didn't fly here to do stuff, meet with you, discuss terms. We didn't spend all this time flying here to do stuff that can be done on Skype. What you're doing here is you're saying, hey, this analyst that you've brought besides chewing up our time with out of phase questions, what value can he add? This is interesting. When I do deals, I bring a team. There's myself, there's my partner, we will have an analyst and then they will bring a team. They may have a couple of analysts, couple of partners. There's lots of people. You guys might be facing person to person selling when they don't have a team. There's just you and them. But they still will be asking all these analytical questions and you're going to treat those questions like the analyst. You're going to do the same thing. Hey, all these questions that you're bringing, they can be done in a breakout session or we can do that later on Skype or by email. Let's focus together, you and I, on the time we have on getting a relationship, making sure you want this product, making sure we would work good together, making sure I want to invest in getting you as a customer. So it doesn't just require teams. You can do this person to person. I've said that you already know how to use this stuff and probably use it daily and I'm going to give you some examples of time frames that you do use and that you do know. If you have to have a meeting with someone and you're a little unsure about the outcome or the value of it, you're going to say, look, I have nine to nine thirty free so let's do a quick call. That's setting a time frame. You're getting control over that situation. They're going to have to move fast, explain what they have quickly and in many ways, supplicate to you and try and win your attention because it's a very short period of time and that communicates through the time frame that you're the prize in many situations. I'll give you another one. If you say to someone, let's do lunch and then go to my office to look at paperwork, that's a very, very different kind of time frame. That's probably two and a half hours with someone and maybe too much time. So in that situation, you're the supplicant. You're trying to spend as much time with them as possible. Very different time frame. Could be the same person and you may on a whim say to that person, I don't know, let's do nine to nine thirty. We may say to the same person, hey, let's do lunch and then go to my office and look at paperwork but those two time frames will have very different outcomes and they are different social communication. I'll give you another one that I'm sure you use. A meeting is going on that was set for an hour. You'd be happy to stay there two hours but you just have something else to do. Every meeting you have to go to with your manager or the CEO or customer. You say, guys, because you have to say it, we have ten minutes left. So let's try and put a fine point on today's discussion. You have to say that and that is a time frame and it makes everybody scramble to summarize and make their points clear to you. So you've used time frames and I'm going to push you to use the same kinds of time frames. When there are not natural constraints, the examples I've given you are natural. You only have a certain amount of time or you have a lot of time. You only have ten minutes. Those are naturally occurring but I'm going to help you push time frames when there are not natural time constraints. You're going to build and manufacture time constraints. It's going to feel a little unnatural because if you say to someone, I see it all the time, you have to decide by Friday and if it's not real, the other person will know it, that they can wait until Monday and it doesn't make a difference. I'm going to help you manufacture real time constraints that are authentic and that you'll stick to but that drive decision making from the other person. Now look, this has nothing to do with car sales. So they say to you, this price is only good by the end of the day. That is a false time constraint. If it's not false, it's aggressive. That is a deadline and we don't use deadlines. We use constraints. So if I meet you and I say, you got to decide by the end of the day or you can't get this deal, that's a time deadline and it may be true but it's way too aggressive for my taste. What I would be more likely to say is, I have today to work with you and the next couple days we need to work together to decide if this is for you or not but definitely by next Monday we have to reach a decision because it can't go beyond Tuesday. That's my constraint. So we're going to teach you how to manufacture these time constraints that feel real and authentic and they're usable. The price frame. At one time or another, you had something that other people wanted and you were the prize. You had a property where there was a bidding war. Everybody wanted it and you got to choose who you were going to let buy it and sometimes maybe it wasn't even the highest price. You just wanted a family to have it because you liked them more or you thought they were going to take better care of what you built or maybe you had a sports talent that different teams wanted and so they were vying to get you on the team. I'll give you a quick little story that's fun. My sister is an excellent ultimate Frisbee player. She can throw this thing like, amazing. And there's a rule in her league that all teams have to have one female. If they have one girl on the team. And there's not a lot of good ultimate Frisbee players. It's really a giveaway position. They get a girl to play and they don't expect that girl to be able to contribute much. Just the way of this sport works. Because my sister is a great ultimate player, everybody wants her on the team because they don't have to throw away the position. She has 10 different teams vying to get her on it and it's awesome for her. In that situation, she's the prize. At some point you may have had companies trying to recruit you all at the same time. And then you weren't worried about what was going to happen. And you weren't supplicating. You were the prize. You felt you had lots of control. You had frame control. Or maybe you had different banks trying to give you a loan. At that time you felt like, hey, I'm the prize. I control this situation. I have frame control. In those situations you were the prize and you did not worry about saying please and thank you or walking on ad shells. You just did whatever you wanted and said, hey, if you don't like this deal, walk because I got five other people who want it. You were the prize. So you felt this before. We got to show you how to manufacture that. You may not have all those people wanting you so aggressively but you still have to be the prize. I thought I was going to say you still have to be the frog but you have to be the prize. So there's a way you'll learn to use frames and you'll do it the same way I did it. You will, number one, use the basic Aristotelian Greek method of learning. And that goes, what do I know? What does it mean? And what should I do? Too frequently we want to move from learning something and deciding what we should do to take an action. So we move to action with insufficient thought. I believe we need a little perspiration and thinking to mix in with the inspiration and excitement of going to do something. Frames are a very provocative subject. We hear about them one time and then we want to rush out and use them. And I think if you take the things that we're talking about here and immediately deploy them, there's a risk of you getting arrested or thrown out of meetings or blowing up deals. And I know that risk is there because I've blown up deals rushing into frame control too quickly. So I recommend an approach now of perceiving. Perceiving and then going to understanding and then taking action. Perceiving, understanding, then taking action. And perceiving, we're immersing ourselves in the material to gain awareness of it and develop a knowledge base. Then we move to this interim phase of understanding where we identify with the material and connect with it. We grasp it with some scope and some depth. Then we apply the knowledge. So it's not going to take long, but we're going to spend some time now in the perceiving model. The purpose of spending time in perceiving is to let your mind confront the ambiguity. I'm telling you to create time frames where you otherwise might not, to become the prize when you don't have the natural power to do it. So you need to confront the ambiguity of what I'm talking about. You need to process the complexity that we're discussing here and see a path through it. I'm going to help you down that path, but your mind has to get a control of it as well. So those who crave action can allow their impatience to dominate their thinking and miss important learning and immersion in the material. And if you move too impatiently to action, you will miss the learning that we're doing now. So keep going with this. The second way you're going to learn this material, it's very simple. There's worksheets. With any new material, do the worksheets. The third way is in the worksheets, there are scripts. These are mad libs. These are things that you can say exactly. They are taken from things I say and do every day. And they're as sophisticated as I am and as good as I am in using time frames, using a prize frame, the power frame, breaking the analyst frame. I can both break the power frame and use it. As good as I am at all those things, I still use these very, very basic scripts. Even though I may be years ahead of you, I still use the scripts that you'll be using yourself. Fill in the blank scripts for the prize frame, the time frame, the power frame, the analyst frame, and the needy frame. And you'll use them exactly as written to practice presenting what you have to sell or to show people your ideas. But use them with friendly people that you know and get accustomed to it. So fourth thing you'll do to learn how to use frames is you'll get into the game in live action fire with real people and real deals. But you'll only start with the prize frame. It's the easiest to do. It's the most natural and people will not respond negatively to it. In addition to prize, you'll be observing and looking for the power and the analyst frames. You'll just be observing and looking for them. Once you become good at identifying them, you'll start to work on counteracting them. So you'll see a power frame and you'll just say, I saw the power frame. The next time you see it, I saw the power frame and I thought to myself, I should do this. Eventually you'll build up the strength that when you see the power frame and you know what you're supposed to do, you'll take that power frame away. We're going to learn how to do that here. But it's important to know the process where you're going to learn this stuff and use it. Number five, and in some ways this is the most important, you'll introduce this material to someone else, a friend or someone you work with closely. So you can have lots of conversations in the language of frames. You'll say to someone, yeah, I definitely got to get the prize frame early. I have to set the big idea because they throw analyst frames very quickly and that whole damn company is a power frame. We got to go in there and crack that nut immediately. You'll talk in the language of frames and your friend or your business associate will know what you mean by all that. That is how I got good at it. Long walks, long lunches, workouts where we're just talking about framing and frame control in this language. It's fun. Nobody goes on a hike or out to coffee for hours talking about calculus. Not nobody. Nobody I know. But economics and about algebra. But frames people do because it's a very, very interesting and fun and social subject. Now we're going to take you into a Q&A discussion I had with a small audience where they asked some very good questions and these are the questions that are likely in your own mind right now. I think you're really going to become immersed in the subject of frames in the next segment when you hear other people asking questions and seeing very complete descriptions of how to use frames and some more definitions. Let's go into the next segment about frames. How do you out frame an expert in your field? I love it. The hardest question I got to get first. This is great. I run into this all the time because as an investment banker I pitch deals that I'm familiar with from a financial standpoint. I know their income statement and balance sheet and statement of cash flows and their revenue pipeline and their upside and downside protection and what their customer contracts look like. But usually I don't know the technology very specifically. So someone will say to me, if I'm pitching a deal someone from the investor side or the buyer side will say to me, hmm, we just don't think those semiconductors can process at a 1,000 micro unit pace and that can't be manufactured at an 80% gross margin. We just don't believe in these technical details you're telling us about. Can you give us proof points? So they challenge you and they bring their experts challenge you on something you don't know. That's the analyst frame, the expert frame and you have to find a way to deal with it. Here's what I do in a very practical way. So that comes at me and I say, guys, listen, we have one hour here, an hour and a half maybe if I give you a little extra time. All these details are in this black binder right here. All the technical specs and it takes hours to pour through them. But I don't think we should waste our time together going through the minutia, the technical details. We would not be here with a $200 million deal. Because hundreds of investors already put money in with customers if these technical specifications were not accurate. So at the right time you bring your analyst and we'll bring our scientists and they'll all sit down in a room and they'll go through the binder and we'll show you exactly that the specifications are accurate. And look, if you want to blow out and do diligence, you find something that's not true or doesn't work, then you blow out and do diligence. There's nothing we can do to stop you. But let's not waste our time here. Time going through the technical details. A, we don't have enough time. B, we don't even know if we'd like each other yet and we don't have terms. So let's not blow the deal up on details, technical details before we even know if a deal can exist. We would not be here lying to you or somehow miscommunicating the product. We wouldn't have gotten this far in the world if that was the way we did things. The black binder, we're all going to go through and do diligence. And again, if you want to blow out, then blow out. But I don't know if I like you yet. Let's figure that out first. So that's how we're going to answer that question. Okay, who's next? Yes, how do you set the frame at a pitch fest or similar cattle call? Yeah, yeah, yeah. Another hard question at the beginning of the question. That's a good one. Many times you have to pitch into a process. A job interview, a pitch fest, a bake off for an advertising agency, or whether it's a process. You don't have control over the process. So in essence, the buyer is interviewing you in little vignettes. And to me, it's like a credit card application. This would be my example. If you're filling out a credit card application, you can't write an essay. You can't attach a USB chip and mail it in and say, look at my video. You can't say, check out my YouTube video that I made on why I should get this credit card. You have to fill in the little forms and you leave out your birthday or your social security number or the last two places that you've lived at. They just chuck it. There's a process. You cannot blow up the process. But what you can do is make sure that if you're there with your time and you've left your work or you've left doing something to be in an interview or a pitch process, you can make sure that you're getting attention. And if there's any sense, because if they put you in a process, then they owe you attention. So if they're glancing at their watch or checking their phone, then because you've agreed to a process, you can turn the axis of power 180 degrees and say, this is a process. If you can't run your own process properly, then what am I doing here? So when there is a process, you're going to require, if you're going to have to live in the process, you're going to require the other people to live in the process. We had a question earlier. Someone says, hey, I'm in an interview process. It's supposed to be just 15 minutes long and they're looking at their phone and sending a text. What do I do? Say you don't allow it. You say excuse me. I left doing something important to come here and help you out. I want to spend this time getting to know each other. And not only are you looking at me, but I'm considering you. I have lots of choices. And as I'm considering you, I'm thinking, wow, she only has to pay attention for 15 minutes. She can't seem to do it. And I'm starting to question myself why I would ever want to work with someone like that. So look, I'm getting off a little bit on the wrong foot. Let's shake the etch-a-sketch upside down and start over. All right? I'm going to go back two minutes and you may have missed that part. And let's go through this process together. You giving me the attention that I deserve for bringing you the chance to look at my experience or my product or whatever it is. And the same token, I'll do my best job to add value and teach you about something you might not already know. And that way, we will have a good relationship. But you checking your phone. It's horrible. OK? So that's a way that we can address that. And that will be effective for you. So, OK, what do we have next? What are the basic framing steps in a deal? OK, basic framing steps. So we've talked about lots of things. My world is usually either a phone call or stepping into a room. So the steps are this. Pricing, number one, pricing. Two, getting a time constraint. So using the time frame. Pricing, getting a time frame. Number three is setting the overall frame by introducing the big idea. OK? And number four is letting people know that at some point, they are going to have to qualify themselves to you. It's part of pricing, but it's a specific step. They're going to have to explain why you would choose them. And a fifth step beyond letting people know they're going to have to qualify back is raising your status. OK? Raising your status. And you will do that by providing value, insight. You have to be providing value the whole time. Value has to come that way. I mean, let me say this in this way. I mean, this is really important. I think I'll say it right here. When you get together with someone else, either on the phone, on the internet, or in person, imagine, oh, I don't know, a tube appears before you guys. And value flows between that tube. Value flows between it. And if you want something from somebody else, you've got to add more value than you take. This is why I hate questions. I don't think I've asked a single question today. I hate questions because they take value. Questions take value. So you want to add value. So through this imaginary tube, you're pushing value. This is what our competitors are doing, and you should call them if you want to get it this way. And there was a recently thing on 60 Minutes about our industry, and we will show you our manufacturing costs so you can understand your really pain for what you get. Oh, and there's a white paper that Gartner put out, and there was an article in the Wall Street Journal, and we'll give you a tour of the factory. And I don't know. We'll give you a free blue plastic frog put in your backyard. Try it out. Just add value. And eventually, the other side will go, I got so much value. I'm going to start giving some back. So that's really that step is to add more value than you're taking. And that will raise your status. I mean, it just makes sense. You add value, and your status goes way up. And then the last step is once your status and your momentum and the intrigue about you is at the highest, and the time frame is counting down. And at that point, you're going to apply that counter pressure and say, this is everything that I have, and it's about to go away. And now you're going to apply to be my customer in the next few minutes. Now, some people have asked, oh my god, that's very strong, and it's aggressive. And I may not even do it like this. It's very easy for me to talk to you right here. I'm not actually selling you an airport or a semiconductor company or a genetics company. I'm not actually selling you so I can talk big, wave my arms around, and point my fingers at you, and sound like a big man. It's easy for me to do here, but do I do it in person? The answer is yes, most of the time I do, but I've been doing it for a long time. You don't have to do it with this level of aggression. But you do have to do the structure. So whatever those five things I just said to do, we'll put them up on the screen. You've got to do those things. Now, you may not do it with such emphatic tone. You might not swear as much as I do. You might not pound the desk or point or color it with reds and blacks and purples. Your colors might be a lighter shade of pan tone, the easier side of the palette. You may be softer talking and you're probably definitely smoother talking than I am. But you have to do these things structurally with whatever kind of tonality you choose. But I can tell you it's a rough world out there deal making and your tonality will get more serious and more aggressive and more compact and forceful the longer you do it because deal making and selling stuff is hard. And you want to be as hard on buyers as they are on you. But then it all kind of ties back together. Remember this is not a financial transaction. The financial transaction happens during due diligence which will happen. This is the social transaction. So yes, you're going to be tough and serious and forceful and get frame control. But you're also going to be having fun. I think again, I'll look here and really maybe this is the biggest point. If you are not having fun, I don't care if you have frame control or not, get out of there. What are you doing? This is about having fun. If you are not having fun, something is wrong. And if you're not having fun, people will sense it. And all these kind of pressures you want to put at them. So if you're not doing it in a social, fun, interesting, light way with some humanity and some humility, then it will feel bitter. So in some ways I'm saying be the prize and tell people that they have to earn the way into your deal. And then I'm saying have some humility. Yes, you're going to do that to give some contrast. You're not going to be over serious. You're not going to walk in and say the first thing, anybody who doesn't do business my way and jump when I say jump and sit when I say sit, I'm not going to do business with them. And they're the buyer. It's not going to work. You've got to move through frame control as a process. We talked about introducing the big idea, introducing the big idea, then focusing on the problem, then focusing on a generic problem, a generic solution, and then your solution. That's a process. In the same way framing yourself up as the prize is a process. You're going to get to your strongest point where you have the most amount of time constraint. Again, I'm just repeat the last step. The most amount of time constraint, the highest status, the most amount of pricing, and you're reaching the end of your presentation and there's most amount of intrigue about you and your product and your company and what you offer. And then at that point when you have maximum power, you're going to make them qualify themselves back to you, explain why they should be allowed to be your customer. Now you've really experienced the process of getting true frame control in a deal. Okay. We'll go to the next question. Or how do you apply this to a one-way medium such as a video sales letter or a webinar? Yeah. Yeah. So this is a great question. So social dynamics, when you can't be social with two people, raises the bar. So what I think to me, the tools you have in frame control and pricing in a sales letter, one-way medium is status, status, novelty, status and novelty. And intrigue and time constraint in a different way. You got to let people know how much of their time you're going to take and to do as much as possible in that period of time. So when you're in a one-way communication, and you see this today, right, in the next five minutes, so people know that they're not going to be trapped in a 45-minute or hour-long or 20-minute-long process, then you're going to add value, not real value. I keep telling internet marketers, right, don't say, I have all this value back here and if you sign up for something, I'll show you a little bit of it. And if you sign up for something else, I'll show you a little bit more. And then, you know, once I have you trapped in this whole big engagement process, I'm going to give you some real value. Give value. That will raise your status, all right. And then eventually, when you feel like you've got a time constraint, you've raised your status, you've added value, right, then you can ask them to do something that's fair. So in a one-way process, I think you're looking for that moment of fairness where I've given you a lot and it's real and I've done it in a credible way. I mean, for me, I'm getting tired of these red circles and giant squeeze pages and headlines with exclamation points and I know, you know, many people listening to this video may do a lot of that, right, but that lowers your status. It's screaming. People are smart, right. Give them real value in a, with some, a novel but credible way and raise your status. See, on the Internet, I see too many people going for control before their status is high enough. Raise your status. Give value under a time constraint. And then when it's fair, ask them to earn their way in a little bit further. You've got to do all those things. Okay. So let's go to another question. Yeah, Orrin, once you've set the frame successfully, what's the best way to inoculate people in the meeting still resistant to it? Oh, such a good question. So I gave a presentation to a board a couple weeks ago and everybody in the room literally went, I mean, these, you know, I'm not there to entertain. I was just helping them understand a different way they could sell their deal. And I finished about a 20 minute presentation and the board clapped. Never had this happen before. Clapped. I mean, it's not, I'm not an entertainer, right. It's amazing. So I must have done some good stuff. But then one guy was watching on Skype, Bill, right, and Bill pipes in from Skype and he says, guys, this is wonderful and everything, but, but I don't get it. What is Orrin here doing? Why would we do things any different from how we've been doing them? Right. So you will always have someone who is kind of not drinking the Kool-Aid. This stuff is very powerful one on one or one on two, but when you get a broader audience, it gets harder. You're not going to capture the attention of everyone. Right. So the answer is when they give you that negative feedback, you don't feed into it. Right. So I said, Bill, I don't know what to tell you. Right. You want me to leave right now? I'll leave. You guys can continue what you've been doing. I'm happy to do it. Right. Just say, Orrin, your head is full of dead insects. I need you to leave right now. I'll do it. No problem. Okay. So you can't feed, you can't validate that or you can't seek validation from that one person. And think about it. If you have buy-in from a room of five, six, eight, ten people and two guys, don't care who they are. Two guys don't believe what you're saying or don't like it, but you have the support and validation of eight people, go with the eight. Leave those two bastards alone. Right. Don't need their validation is the answer. So the way you deal with this is you go with the approval that you're getting and the relationships that you have. Those people will come along or not, but don't seek, don't need validation from them and don't go seeking it out. Go with your strengths, not with your weaknesses when you're in a room. Lauren, so what if you're in an interview where there's a specific process to follow, meaning the interview itself is not free for them? Yeah. Yeah. So this is similar to the other question, you know, is what if you are in a, you know, a pitch process, but interviews many times are not free for them. You know, they have some questions for you. There's an application process. All right. So I kind of think in these situations, you have to think of it as gulliver's travels, maybe. I just thinking of it because they just had that commercial on, the big guy. You know, when they trap gulliver, they do it with these tiny little strands. They don't do it with one big chain or rope. So when you have a process to follow, you follow the process. You can't disrupt other people's processes. They will eject you. But you're going to do this framework in little pieces during the process and it will accumulate. So let's talk about it. If they say, if they say, we have 30 minutes for the process or the interview, you say, really? That's funny because I only have 29. Right? Get a little thread down to try and get gulliver tied down. Right? You're going to, so that's getting some, you know, getting some control over the time frame. You might say something like, I'm glad I was able to come here and help you guys have a successful process. Right? It's another little strand. Right? I think you could say something like, when you're finished your process, there's something really interesting I want to tell you about your company that I've learned from just being here. Right? So there's an intrigue frame that you set in place that's going to make them want to leave some extra time outside of the process. Right? I think that's definitely, when somebody has a process, that's definitely a power frame. It's definitely a power frame. Right? So one way you can take away a power frame is say, you know, money means nothing to me. I have plenty of it. You know, you may only have $1,000. I might be plenty of money. You might have millions. You say, I have plenty of money. Right? What I'm really interested in is to work with good people that are having fun and doing the Lord's work, you know, or whatever it is. So people who are doing good stuff, contributing to society, you know, so now you're applying, you know, another thin strand of the morality frame. You don't care about the money. Right? It means nothing to me. What I care about is good people doing good stuff, helping other people, being authentic, transparent, you know, all these things. So raise the bar. When you do that, they're going to blow up their own process a little bit, proving themselves back to you. So I'm trying to think when you're in a process, some other little kind of strands, you understand? You're not going to blow their process up, but you're going to kind of set these frames and they will start to, their own process will start to erode as they want to come into your process because it's better. It's better. Right? You're talking about intrigue and relationships and doing good stuff and money doesn't matter and who's the prize and who's commodity and these are existential things and they're probably focused on the veneer and some questions and you're focused on things that are lasting and ephemeral and fundamental to human relationships and when you do it that way, they're going to, you know, they're going to come over into the way you do things. So that's how you do it with a process. I know I can break, except for a credit card application. I can't do anything with that, but I know I can break anybody's process. I challenge anybody to come on. I can break their process because their process is going to be based on information and information is never, information is never going to be more important than the thin line that goes through the middle of the human heart that rides between good and evil, right? Right and wrong and when you can bring these things into a discussion, it's always going to come your way because information is nothing. It needs to be framed. Some ways I think about a building, right? A building is nothing without the people who will go in it, either rent offices or buy retail products or use it as a hospital. I mean, it's just a building means nothing without people. You know, a lot of times people will say, well, I'm going to tell my story, right? I say, well, that's not a goddamn story you're telling. It's not a story because it has no characters, right? It's not what happens. It's a story is who it happens to. So when you're in a process and you make the who things are happening to more important than the what of their questions, then they will abandon their process and come over to the way you do things which are better. I think that's going to be how you deal with the process. So I said a swear word and the customer seemed shocked. I felt like I really lost the frame. Yeah, yeah. All right. So you, you were talking to someone and you got excited, right? And you said shit or damn it. Or I don't know what we can say here. And they got mad at you. They said, hey, this is a business environment. Can't swear like that. We're upset. Right? You say to them, have you lost your mind right now? We're talking about starting a company. We're going to be dealing with millions of dollars. Customers are not going to pay their bills. Employees are going to steal from us. Right? Our CFO is going to leave at the last minute before we file for our IPO registration. Our building is going to leak water on all of our expensive computer equipment and our backups are going to take two weeks to bring online and we're going to have to solve this. You know how the hard work we're going to have to do together? Can you imagine? I'm going to be staying up late nights and weekends and working with people I may not even like for a while.
